params.text:
People are more than one label

The Equality Act lists nine protected characteristics, but no one is defined by a single one. A person is never just their age, or just their religion — they hold several identities at the same time. Intersectionality is the idea that these identities overlap and shape a person’s experience together.

For example, a young disabled woman may face barriers that are different from those met by a disabled man, or by a woman who is not disabled. Her experience is not simply disability added to gender; the two combine in ways that are unique to her.

Why this matters in your care

If you think about only one part of who someone is, you can miss the barriers that really affect them. An older gay man may worry about being open with staff; a Black woman may have had past experiences that make her wary of services. Seeing the whole person helps you give care that truly fits.

  • See the whole person: notice how someone’s identities work together, not in isolation.
  • Avoid assumptions: two people who share one characteristic are not the same.
  • Listen first: let each person tell you what matters most to them.

params.question: Intersectionality means that a person’s overlapping identities — such as age, race, disability and gender — combine to shape their experience, so you should see the whole person rather than a single characteristic.

l10n.wrongAnswerMessage: Not quite. Intersectionality is about identities overlapping and combining, so focusing on just one characteristic can hide the barriers a person really faces.
l10n.correctAnswerMessage: Correct. Identities overlap and interact, so recognising the whole person helps you spot the barriers that truly affect them.
